skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: Mask-driven image inpainting on RunComfy via the runcomfy CLI. Routes to Tongyi MAI Z-Image Turbo Inpainting when a mask is available, and to identity-preserving edit models (Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, FLUX Kontext Pro) when the region must be described. Use for object removal, watermark removal, region replacement, and blemish cleanup.
skill.summary: Mask-driven image inpainting using binary masks with the runcomfy CLI
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: All 103 static findings evaluated as false positives. External_commands are markdown code examples, network endpoints are legitimate RunComfy service URLs, weak_crypto flags are model identifier misclassifications, and filesystem access is documented token storage. No malicious intent detected. Security documentation is thorough and includes appropriate warnings against arbitrary install scripts.

content.user_title: Remove objects and fill image regions with AI inpainting
content.value_statement: Need to remove unwanted objects from photos or fill masked regions with AI-generated content. This skill uses mask-driven inpainting via the runcomfy CLI to precisely edit specific areas of images.
actual_capabilities: Remove objects from images using binary mask targeting
Replace masked regions with AI-generated content matching the surroundings
Remove watermarks and blemishes with precision control
Use description-based fallback when no mask is available
Control inpainting strength from subtle retouching to full replacement
Apply brand-style LoRA adapters for consistent inpainting aesthetics
limitations: Requires a mask or detailed spatial description to identify the target region
Cannot extend the canvas beyond the original image boundaries (see outpainting skill)
Does not generate masks; external tools like Photoshop or segment-anything models are needed
Results depend on mask quality; blurry edges (1-3px) produce better blends

use_cases.title: Product photography cleanup
use_cases.description: Remove background distractions, price tags, or unwanted reflections from product photos while preserving the main subject and brand aesthetics.

use_cases.title: Historical photo restoration
use_cases.description: Remove scratches, stains, or damaged sections from old photographs by masking the damaged area and letting AI reconstruct the missing content.

use_cases.title: UI screenshot refinement
use_cases.description: Clean up screenshots by removing sensitive data, test artifacts, or UI elements that should not appear in documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic object removal with mask URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the [object description] from this image using the mask at [mask_url]. Preserve [surrounding elements]. Strength 0.5.
prompt_templates.scenario: User provides both image URL and mask URL

prompt_templates.title: Watermark removal with spatial description
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the watermark in the [location description]. Keep everything else exactly as in the input. Use description-based edit since no mask is available.
prompt_templates.scenario: No mask available, must use spatial language

prompt_templates.title: Background replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the background with [description]. Use mask-driven inpainting with strength 0.9 for full background swap.

prompt_templates.title: Brand-style inpainting with LoRA
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove [object] and fill with [description] using the brand-style LoRA to maintain [brand aesthetic] consistency.
prompt_templates.scenario: Brand-consistent inpainting with trained LoRA adapter

output_examples.input: Remove the person in the background of this vacation photo. Mask covers the person. Preserve the main subject and the ocean view.
output_examples.output: The background person has been removed. The mask area now shows a natural continuation of the ocean and sky, with the main subject preserved intact. Strength was set to 0.6 for clean blending with existing content.

output_examples.input: Remove the timestamp watermark in the bottom-right corner of this screenshot.
output_examples.output: The timestamp watermark has been removed using description-based inpainting. The bottom-right corner now shows the original background content (white wall) as if the timestamp was never there.

output_examples.input: Replace the cluttered background with a solid light gray studio backdrop. Mask the background only.
output_examples.output: The background has been replaced with a smooth light gray studio paper backdrop. The mask precisely targeted the background area while preserving the main subject. Strength 0.9 used for complete background replacement.

best_practices: Use grayscale masks with white representing the inpaint region and black for preservation. Apply slight blur (1-3px) to mask edges for smoother blending.
Set strength based on intent: 0.3-0.5 for subtle retouching, 0.6-0.7 for style-matched replacement, 0.8-1.0 for full region replacement.
Include preservation constraints in prompts: describe what should remain unchanged (e.g., preserve rooflines, match brick pattern, keep sky gradient).
anti_patterns: Do not use inpainting for outpainting tasks; use the image-outpainting skill to extend canvas beyond original boundaries.
Avoid vague spatial descriptions without a mask; provide precise mask URLs when available for better results.
Do not skip mask provenance verification; ensure the user actually wants the masked region replaced, not a mask from a different image.

faq.question: What is the difference between mask-driven and description-based inpainting?
faq.answer: Mask-driven inpainting uses a binary grayscale mask to precisely define the edit region. Description-based inpainting uses spatial language to identify the target area when no mask is available.

faq.question: How do I create a mask for inpainting?
faq.answer: Use image editing tools like Photoshop, GIMP, or segment-anything models. Create a grayscale image where white pixels mark the area to inpaint and black pixels mark areas to preserve.

faq.question: What strength value should I use?
faq.answer: Use 0.3-0.5 for subtle retouching and blemish cleanup. Use 0.6-0.7 for object replacement with style matching. Use 0.8-1.0 for complete region replacement.

faq.question: Can this skill extend image boundaries (outpainting)?
faq.answer: No. This skill is for inpainting within the original image boundaries. Use the image-outpainting skill to extend the canvas beyond the original image.

faq.question: What models are used for inpainting?
faq.answer: Z-Image Turbo Inpainting is the default for mask-driven edits. For description-based edits without a mask, Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, or FLUX Kontext Pro are used.

faq.question: Is my image data sent to external servers?
faq.answer: Yes, images are sent to the RunComfy API (model-api.runcomfy.net) for processing. The security section documents allowed endpoints and data handling practices.
